skill.name: azure-ai-contentsafety-ts
skill.description: Analyze text and images for harmful content using Azure AI Content Safety REST SDK for TypeScript, including severity categories and custom blocklists.
skill.summary: Build TypeScript content moderation workflows with Azure AI Content Safety.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic risk because markdown examples include Azure network calls, environment-variable credentials, file reads, and many fenced code blocks. Manual review found documentation-only TypeScript examples for Azure AI Content Safety, with no prompt injection, no hidden execution path, and no evidence of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Network Configuration
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE but low risk: the skill documents Azure Content Safety endpoints and a sample Blob Storage URL. These network references match the advertised moderation workflow and do not show unauthorized third-party exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are visible in the markdown and are normal Azure service examples. I found no evidence that secrets or unrelated local data are sent to an unexpected host.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Variables in Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE but low risk: the examples read CONTENT_SAFETY_ENDPOINT and CONTENT_SAFETY_KEY to authenticate with Azure. This is expected SDK usage, but users should keep keys in secure secret storage and avoid logging them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The process.env references are explicit and limited to Azure endpoint and credential setup. No code in the skill reads arbitrary environment variables or transmits the key outside the Azure client setup.

low_findings.title: Local Image File Read Example
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE but low risk: the image analysis example reads a local image file and converts it to base64 for Azure analysis. This is expected for image moderation, but users should only analyze files they intend to send to Azure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is a fixed example path for image moderation, not a recursive or hidden file collection pattern. The operational privacy risk is real because image bytes are prepared for cloud analysis.

low_findings.title: Fenced Code Blocks Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the many external command findings are markdown fenced code blocks and TypeScript template literals, not Ruby shell backtick execution. The only shell command shown is a standard npm install command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the flagged backticks are markdown fences or TypeScript interpolation examples. There is no executable Ruby code or instruction to run arbitrary shell commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the weak cryptography scanner matches occur in descriptive moderation text, sample blocklist terms, template-string output, and markdown tables. No cryptographic API or hashing operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation strings and markdown table content. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation in the skill.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the scanner combined documentation examples for Azure credentials, network calls, and image file reading into a critical heuristic. Manual review found no hidden workflow, prompt injection, or malicious instruction to collect and exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky capabilities appear only as transparent SDK examples for the stated Azure Content Safety use case. No prompt-injection language or deceptive authority claims were found during review.

content.user_title: Moderate Content with Azure AI Safety
content.value_statement: Teams need consistent checks for harmful text and images before content reaches users. This skill shows how to use Azure AI Content Safety from TypeScript with categories, severity levels, and blocklists.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation for the Azure Content Safety REST SDK and related Azure authentication packages.
Demonstrates API key and DefaultAzureCredential client setup for TypeScript projects.
Provides examples for text analysis across hate, sexual, violence, and self-harm categories.
Shows image moderation with base64 content and Blob Storage URL inputs.
Documents custom blocklist creation, item management, listing, deletion, and analysis integration.
Includes a reusable moderation helper that returns allow status, flagged categories, severity, and blocklist matches.
limitations: Requires an Azure AI Content Safety resource and valid credentials.
Examples are guidance snippets, not a complete application or deployable service.
Moderation thresholds must be adapted to the product policy and jurisdiction.
Image examples may send image content or URLs to Azure for analysis.

use_cases.title: Moderate Community Posts
use_cases.description: Add TypeScript checks for user text before posts, comments, or messages become visible.

use_cases.title: Review Uploaded Images
use_cases.description: Analyze submitted images with Azure severity categories before routing them to approval queues.

use_cases.title: Manage Product Blocklists
use_cases.description: Create and update custom blocklists that supplement AI moderation with product-specific prohibited terms.

prompt_templates.title: Start Text Moderation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to set up Azure AI Content Safety in a TypeScript project and analyze one text string for all harm categories.

prompt_templates.title: Add Image Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the Azure Content Safety image example so my TypeScript service can review an uploaded image before publishing it.

prompt_templates.title: Design Blocklist Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript workflow for creating a custom blocklist, adding terms, and using it during text moderation.

prompt_templates.title: Build Moderation Helper
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a reusable TypeScript moderation helper with configurable severity thresholds, blocklist names, and audit logging.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to moderate a comment before saving it.
output_examples.output: The response outlines Azure client setup, text analysis categories, threshold handling, and a decision flow for allow, review, or block.

output_examples.input: A trust and safety analyst asks how blocklists fit into AI moderation.
output_examples.output: The response explains creating a named blocklist, adding prohibited terms, checking matches, and combining matches with severity results.

output_examples.input: A product team asks how to handle image uploads.
output_examples.output: The response describes using base64 or Blob URL image inputs, reviewing category severity, and protecting user privacy when sending files to Azure.

best_practices: Store Azure keys in a managed secret store and rotate them on a regular schedule.
Tune severity thresholds by category and route borderline cases to human review.
Log moderation decisions without storing sensitive content longer than necessary.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure keys or resource endpoints in source control.
Do not apply one severity threshold to every product area without policy review.
Do not send private images or text to Azure unless users and policies allow it.

faq.question: What Azure package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure AI Content Safety REST SDK for TypeScript, published as @azure-rest/ai-content-safety.

faq.question: Can it analyze both text and images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes text analysis examples and image analysis examples using base64 content or a Blob Storage URL.

faq.question: Does it include authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows API key authentication and DefaultAzureCredential setup for Azure environments.

faq.question: Can I use custom blocklists?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents creating blocklists, adding items, listing them, deleting them, and using them during analysis.

faq.question: Is this a full moderation product?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with implementation examples that you must adapt to your application and policy requirements.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review credential storage, privacy requirements, Azure data handling, severity thresholds, and human review workflows.
